data.title: A Quiet Friday Leaves Yields Higher on the Week — Market Talk
data.description:
1546 ET - Friday data, including an in-line PCE inflation report and a better-than-expected consumer-confidence survey from the University of Michigan, did little to swing the Treasury market. Traders have been in a holding pattern ahead of the Fed's final 2025 meeting, where the dot plot will provide an updated look at how Fed officials anticipate steering interest rates in 2026. The calendar is quiet ahead of the Fed meeting, although Tuesday will bring a delayed September JOLTS release that could update investors' views on how well the labor market has held up. The benchmark 10-year yield rose 12 basis points this week to end at 4.138%, while the 2-year yield climbed by 7 bps and ended at 3.562%. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1444 ET - The Canadian dollar appreciates nearly 1% versus USD, as traders believe further Bank of Canada rate cuts are no longer in the cards after a third-straight month of strong job gains. Some economists and traders are now musing about possible interest-rate increases in 2H of 2026, given recent GDP-data revisions that suggest Canada's economy was in far better shape than previously believed. USDCAD is down 0.90%, or its lowest level since late September. This leaves Canada's dollar on track for an appreciation of almost 4% since the end of last year, albeit from weak levels, says BMO Capital Markets chief economist Doug Porter. CAD's performance is relatively solid amid elevated trade-policy turbulence during 2025. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1330 ET - Canada's labor-market data, while surprising on the upside over the past three months, still indicates ample spare capacity in the economy, says Avery Shenfeld, chief economist at CIBC Capital Markets. In his week-ending note to clients, he says Statistics Canada's recent revisions to GDP levels indicate there's more production capacity in the economy than initially believed. Regardless, Shenfeld says the labor market, and unemployment rate, is a more appropriate gauge of slack — and at 6.5%, that remains elevated and could fall further. "We have a higher current level of GDP, but also more room to have additional, non-inflationary output if we could put more Canadians to work," Shenfeld says. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1311 ET - Canada's more timely, more closely watched employment data sent jaws dropping with a 53,600 jobs gain in November and steep 0.4-percentage-point drop in the unemployment rate. Some economists worry that the labor-force survey, or LFS, results aren't relaying the true story of Canada's labor market, as they don't match with Statistics Canada's less timely payroll data, known as SEPH. The comparisons, indeed, are stark. The rolling LFS 3-month average rose to 60,200 in November. In contrast, the rolling 3-month average for payroll data, covering the July-to-September period, indicate employment shrank by about 5,000. The LFS's six-month and 12-month averages indicate employment increases of 26,000 and 29,000, respectively. The payroll data indicate much softer employment growth over comparable time periods, of 1,500 on average over six months and 1,100 on average over the past year of data. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1247 ET - There are hopes three months makes a trend when it comes to Canadian employment, after hiring last month again came in considerably stronger than expected. One trend Indeed's Brendon Bernard wants to see continue is in youth jobs. The chief economist for the online employment site notes that amid the monthly volatility in labor market data was a sharp decline in the youth unemployment rate to 12.8% in November from 14.1%. That remains elevated, and well above the national level and for other age ground, but is positive, Bernard says. He says that if the improvement continues, even amid an otherwise lukewarm economic situation, it could signal the slowdown in population growth from abroad is contributing to improving youth employment rates.(robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1235 ET - National Bank Financial is among those expressing doubt about the veracity of Canada's jobs data for November. The economy added 53,600, Statistics Canada says and the unemployment rate fell to a 16-month low. "We are not entirely confident that the Canadian labor market has turned the corner," the firm's strategists say. They cite ongoing trade tensions, which is squeezing the manufacturing sector. NBF also cites mixed signals from various indicators — for instance, S&P Global data that indicated a contraction in the Canadian services and manufacturing sectors in November. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1225 ET - Financial markets may be coming around to Scotiabank economist Derek Holt's thinking: that the next move from the Bank of Canada could be a rate hike. Canada's labor market is on fire, Holt notes, with another about 54,000 jobs added last month to take the rise in three months to 181,000 and sending the unemployment rate down to 6.5%. Short term bond yields jumped following the data, indicating investors are pricing in a possible interest rate rise in September or October, which Holt says is in keeping with his forecast a half-percentage point will added to rates in the second half of 2016. Most economists at the other big domestic banks pencil in an extended hold for the central bank through 2026. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1149 ET - Gold prices are broadly flat in evening trade, with New York futures at $4,244.70 a troy ounce. The Federal Reserve's favored inflation gauge stayed under 3% in September, showing a moderate month-to-month rise in prices that is unlikely to prevent policymakers from considering an interest-rate cut at next week's meeting. Meanwhile, consumer sentiment improved slightly in December. "An interest rate cut is now considered a certainty again. However, the interest rate outlook is of greater interest," Barbara Lambrecht from Commerzbank. "If the FOMC members expect more interest rate cuts compared to September, this could push the gold price even higher, especially as interest rate cuts are hardly priced in for the first Fed meetings in the new year." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1148 ET - Netflix is taking on nearly $61 billion in debt in its $72 billion acquisition of Warner Bros. Netflix says it will use $10.3 billion in cash on hand and borrow $50 billion to fund the cash portion of the takeover. It will also shell out roughly $11.7 billion in stock to Warner investors and absorb Warner's $10.7 billion in studios and streaming debt in the deal, which carries an enterprise value of $82.7 billion. Netflix says it's committed to maintaining solid investment-grade credit ratings through rapid post-close debt reduction. Netflix slips 2.7%, while Warner is up 3.1%. (colin.kellaher@wsj.com)

1044 ET - Part-time job creation powered the surprisingly positive results from Canada's November employment report, which might prompt some market watchers to be skeptical about the labor market's underlying strength. Andrew Hencic, economist at TD Bank, says however it's tough to ignore the sharp drop in the unemployment rate to a 16-month low. "The takeaway has to be that the Canadian labor market is in better shape than most had thought," Hencic says. "That said, this situation can't be characterized as 'good.'" He says the unemployment rate, at 6.5%, still remains elevated — or at 5.6% using U.S. Labor Department methodology — so slack does remain. "There is still room for recovery." (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1035 ET - U.S. investor home purchases rose 1% year over year in 3Q, Redfin says coming in at a total of roughly 52,000. Investor activity has flattened because market conditions are essentially the opposite of those that fueled the pandemic investment boom, and the current environment means many buyers are priced out of the market. Investors face additional roadblocks, including less potential to make a profit by flipping or renting out a property. More homes are selling at a loss, and the rental market has softened. Investors aren't completely retreating, but they're not driving the housing market forward. Real estate investors purchased 17% of U.S. homes that sold in 3Q, up from 16% a year earlier. Investors aren't expecting the double-digit appreciation common during the pandemic homebuying boom. Also, rent growth has cooled, and vacancies are rising. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1031 ET - Oxford Economics casts a skeptical eye toward Statistics Canada's November jobs report, which indicates employment rose by a much bigger-than-anticipated 53,600. Tony Stillo, Oxford's head of Canada economics, says the recent pickup in hiring based on the data agency's labor-force survey doesn't jive with Statistics Canada's less timely, "but more well grounded" payroll report. The payroll data for September, published last week, suggests employers shed 58,000 jobs in September, and that the year-over-year increase was a tepid 0.1%. Stillo says he doesn't believe this pace of hiring can be sustained and expects layoffs to build in the near term. Just this week, Algoma Steel said it would eliminate 1,000 jobs, citing in part President Trump's tariff policy. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

data.title: Thor Industries Is Maintained at Neutral by Citigroup
HORMEL FOODS CORP /DE/ SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Hormel Foods Corporation, a leading player in the meat and food products industry, has released its Form 10-K report for fiscal 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in a competitive market. Hormel Foods operates through three main segments: Retail, Foodservice, and International, with a significant presence in markets such as China and Brazil. The company continues to focus on product innovation, supply chain optimization, and strategic investments to drive long-term growth.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Sales: $12,106 million, reflecting a 1.6% increase from the prior year driven by growth across all segments.
  • Gross Profit: $1,892 million, a decrease of 6.4% from the previous year due to inflationary pressures despite cost savings from the T&M initiative.
  • Operating Income: $719 million, a decline of 32.7% compared to the prior year, impacted by non-cash impairment charges.
  • Net Earnings Attributable to Hormel Foods Corporation: $478 million, a decrease of 40.6% from the previous year, primarily due to impairment charges and higher effective tax rates.
  • Diluted Earnings Per Share: $0.87, down from $1.47 in the prior year, reflecting the decrease in net earnings.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: Hormel Foods operates through three main segments: Retail, Foodservice, and International. The Retail segment includes brands like Jennie-O®, Planters®, and Applegate®, while the Foodservice segment focuses on customized solutions and premium prepared proteins. The International segment covers markets such as China and Brazil, with products like SPAM® and Planters®.
  • Geographical Performance: The company has a significant presence in international markets, including China, Brazil, and the Philippines. The China market showed volume and net sales growth, while Brazil faced competitive pressures affecting performance.
  • Sales Units: For fiscal 2025, the company reported a decline in volume across segments, with Retail and International segments experiencing a decrease in sales units. The Foodservice segment saw organic volume growth due to strong performance in customized solutions and premium prepared proteins.
  • New Production Launches: Hormel Foods invested in capacity expansions, including projects in Barron, Wisconsin, and Jiaxing, China. These expansions are part of the company's strategy to support growth and innovation.
  • Future Outlook: The company anticipates organic net sales growth of 1% to 4% in fiscal 2026, driven by increased brand support, innovation, and market-based pricing actions. Segment profit growth is expected across all segments, with risks including inflationary pressures and macroeconomic conditions.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Transform and Modernize (T&M) Initiative: The company continued its multi-year T&M initiative, focusing on supply chain and portfolio optimization to deliver long-term value. This initiative includes project-based external consulting fees and expenses related to asset write-offs, severance, and relocation costs.
  • Corporate Restructuring Plan: The company commenced a corporate restructuring plan aimed at reducing administrative expenses, improving efficiencies, and aligning the workforce with future needs.
  • Capital Management: The company maintained a disciplined capital allocation strategy, prioritizing core uses of cash such as capital expenditures, dividend payments, and debt repayments. In fiscal 2025, capital expenditures were $311 million, focusing on capacity expansions, data and technology investments, and safety improvements. The company paid $633 million in dividends, marking the 60th consecutive year of dividend increases. Additionally, the company repaid $950 million of senior unsecured notes upon maturity and issued $500 million in new long-term debt. The company did not repurchase any shares during fiscal 2025 but remains authorized to repurchase 3,677,494 shares under its existing plan.
  • Future Outlook: For fiscal 2026, the company expects to continue supporting growth through increased marketing and advertising investments, with capital expenditures estimated between $260 million and $290 million. The company plans to maintain its dividend growth strategy, with an implied annualized dividend rate of $1.17 per share. The company anticipates year-over-year growth in volume, net sales, and segment profit across its segments, while managing risks related to inflationary pressures and macroeconomic conditions. The company remains committed to its T&M initiative, which is expected to cease at the end of the investment period, and plans to explore strategic investments and acquisitions aligned with its priorities.

Challenges and Risks

  • Competitive Market: Hormel Foods faces significant challenges in maintaining its competitive position in the highly competitive meat and food products industry. The company competes with national and regional producers of various protein sources, including pork, turkey, beef, chicken, fish, nuts, and plant-based proteins. Key competitive factors include price, product quality, brand identification, and customer service.
  • Economic Conditions: Economic conditions, such as inflation, interest rates, and availability of capital, could harm the company's financial stability.
  • Acquisitions and Divestitures: The company faces risks associated with acquisitions, joint ventures, and divestitures, including integration challenges and potential impairment charges.
  • Intangible Asset Impairments: Intangible asset impairments, particularly in goodwill and trade names, pose a risk to financial results.
  • Operational Disruptions: Disruptions in operations, whether due to facility issues or third-party service provider challenges, could negatively impact the company’s ability to operate effectively.
  • Cybersecurity Threats: Cybersecurity threats and IT system disruptions are significant risks, especially with increased reliance on technology and remote work.
  • Labor Relations: Labor relations and availability, along with rising labor costs, could impact operations and financial performance.
  • Food Safety and Disease Outbreaks: The company also faces risks related to food safety, disease outbreaks among livestock, and fluctuations in commodity prices, which could affect supply and production costs.
  • Climate Change: Climate change presents both physical and regulatory risks, potentially impacting raw material availability and operational costs.
  • Legal and Regulatory Risks: Legal and regulatory risks, including compliance with environmental laws and international trade barriers, could result in increased costs and decreased revenues.
  • Market Risks: Hormel Foods is exposed to market risks, including fluctuations in commodity prices and foreign currency exchange rates. The company uses hedging instruments to manage some of these risks, but market volatility could still impact financial results. The company’s exposure to interest rate fluctuations could also affect borrowing costs and liquidity.

data.title: Fitch Ratings: High Costs, ACA Disruption Drive Deteriorating Outlook for US Health Insurers in 2026
data.description:
Related Content: U.S. Health Insurance Outlook 2026

Fitch Ratings-Chicago/New York-05 December 2025: Persistent, elevated medical cost trends and regulatory uncertainty will continue to challenge U.S. health insurers across nearly all major lines of business in 2026, according to Fitch Ratings’ 2026 annual outlook report. Fitch currently maintains a ‘deteriorating’ sector outlook.

Senior Director Brad Ellis highlights ongoing sector headwinds: “Despite the potential for operating performance improvement among some participants in the health insurance sector in 2026, continued high utilization trends, disruption in the ACA Marketplace, and ongoing legislative and regulatory developments suggest a very challenging environment over the coming 12 months.”

Elevated utilization trends are expected to remain a defining industry headwind, driven by rising provider visits, higher unit costs, and increased demand for specialty pharmacy and behavioral health services. The likely expiration of the enhanced premium tax credits at the close of 2025 is set to drive considerable disruption in the ACA exchange marketplace, with risk pool deterioration and adverse selection pressuring profitability. Insurers are actively reevaluating their future participation in some markets as they prepare for these shifts.

Medicare Advantage continues to experience substantial disruption as recent regulatory changes, benefit structure adjustments, and the phase-in of the CMS risk adjustment model contribute to margin pressures. Pharmacy spend, particularly for specialty drugs and GLP-1 therapies, is accelerating across both Medicare Advantage and employer-sponsored commercial group plans. Medicaid faces additional challenges: the U.S. Tax and Spending Bill will result in stricter eligibility requirements, potentially before year-end 2026 in some states, lower enrollment and reduced premium revenue, while inadequate state reimbursement rates and rising acuity among remaining enrollees further strain margins.

Management teams are prioritizing core business stability, with subdued M&A activity expected to persist. Fitch anticipates continued margin pressure in 2026, with the possibility of further rating downgrades where operating performance does not improve.

data.title: MGT CAPITAL INVESTMENTS, INC. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
MGT Capital Investments, Inc., a company engaged in Bitcoin mining and hosting services, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report highlights significant changes in the company's financial and operational performance, primarily driven by the cessation of self-mining activities and the sale of key assets.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenue: $0 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $88 million for the same period in 2024, reflecting a 100% decrease due to the cessation of self-mining activities in March 2025.
  • Operating Loss: $(187) thousand for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $(335) thousand for the same period in 2024, indicating a reduction in operating expenses primarily due to decreased electricity costs and general administrative expenses.
  • Net Income: $446 thousand for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $5,272 thousand for the same period in 2024, primarily driven by a gain on the sale of property and equipment.
  • Basic Net Income Per Share: $0.00 for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $0.01 for the same period in 2024, reflecting the impact of reduced revenue and operating activities.
  • Diluted EPS: $0.00 for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $0.01 for the same period in 2024, consistent with the basic net income per share due to the anti-dilutive effect of potential securities.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The company's revenue during the reporting period was derived from two primary sources: Bitcoin mining and hosting services. The hosting services involved providing capacity for third-party miners, while Bitcoin mining was conducted using company-owned machines.
  • Operational Changes: In March 2025, the company ceased its self-mining activities and its lease with the primary hosting customer expired. This led to a significant reduction in operational activities related to digital asset mining.
  • Asset Management: Following the cessation of mining operations, the company sold its LaFayette, Georgia facility in May 2025. The sale included land, containers, and electrical infrastructure, which were part of the company's former hosting and mining operations.
  • Future Outlook: The company is currently focused on preserving liquidity and evaluating strategic alternatives to monetize or repurpose its existing assets. Management is exploring new business opportunities in sectors aligned with its historical expertise, such as digital assets, financial technology, and data infrastructure.
  • Strategic Focus: Post-sale of its mining facility, the company is restructuring its balance sheet and evaluating new business opportunities that leverage its public-company platform. Potential areas under evaluation include financial technology, blockchain infrastructure services, and digital-asset management.

data.title: Treasuries See Further Downside Even As Inflation Data Meets Estimates
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Following the notable pullback seen during the previous session, treasuries saw continued weakness during trading on Friday.

Bond prices fluctuated early in the session but slid firmly into negative territory as the day progressed. Subsequently, the yield on the benchmark ten-year note, which moves opposite of its price, rose 3.1 basis points to 4.139 percent.

The continued weakness among treasuries came even though the Commerce Department released closely watched consumer price inflation data that largely came in line with economist estimates.

The Commerce Department said its personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index climbed by 0.3 percent in September, matching the growth seen in August along with economist estimates.

The annual rate of growth by the PCE price index ticked up to 2.8 percent in September from 2.7 percent in August, which was also in line with expectations.

Excluding food and energy prices, the core PCE price index rose by 0.2 percent in September, matching the increases seen in the two previous months as well as economist estimates.

The annual rate of growth by the core PCE price index edged down to 2.8 percent in September from 2.9 percent in August. Economists had expected the annual rate of growth to remain unchanged.

The unexpected slowdown in the annual rate of core price growth has reinforced recent optimism about the outlook interest rates ahead of next week's Federal Reserve meeting.

However, a report from the University of Michigan showing a bigger than expected rebound by U.S. consumer sentiment in December may have reduced treasuries' safe haven appeal.

The University of Michigan said its consumer sentiment index climbed to 53.3 in December from 51.0 in November. Economists had expected the consumer sentiment index to rise to 52.0.

The bigger than expected rebound by the consumer sentiment index came after it dropped to its lowest level since hitting a record low of 50.0 in June 2022.

Next week's trading is likely to be driven by reaction to the Fed's monetary policy decision as well as any hints about the outlook for interest rates.

data.title: When the Architecture Becomes Visible: The SMX Revaluation Explained
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / December 5, 2025 / Markets have a history of overlooking foundational technology until the moment they cannot. It happened with semiconductors. It happened with mobile operating systems. It happened with encrypted payments. In each case, the market understood the products long before it understood the architecture that made the products possible. When the architecture finally came into focus, valuation frameworks changed almost overnight. And valuations in those companies bringing it soared.

SMX (NASDAQ:SMX) is entering that same moment. For years, the company was viewed as a niche player in recycling, a traceability tool for metals, or an ESG reporting enhancer. That is not unusual. Markets tend to assign narrow categories to technologies they do not yet understand. What changed is that several industries, all at once, discovered that SMX was not sitting inside their systems. It was powering the parts of those systems that had never worked correctly.

Gold exposed the first fracture. For generations, the bullion world accepted a simple reality. Heat erases history. A melted bar forgets where it came from. Paperwork tries to replace identity, but paperwork has limitations that every regulator understands. SMX broke that historical rule by giving the metal a memory that survives smelting, reshaping, vault rotation, and transport. Suddenly, the industry realized it had been solving authentication with the wrong layer of technology. The engine needed to be inside the material, not outside it.

Rare Earths, ESG, and Digital, Too

Rare earth minerals brought a second discovery. These materials support national defense, clean energy infrastructure, robotics, and aerospace. Entire nations depend on their supply. Yet once ore enters processing, origin becomes impossible to verify. Governments have treated this opacity as an unavoidable characteristic of the industry. SMX replaced inevitability with identity. Rare earths could now move from mine to magnet with origin preserved. The technology solves a geopolitical verification problem that no documentation system has ever touched.

ESG systems revealed a third discovery. Sustainability claims have matured faster than the tools available to prove them. Material recovery, recycled content, and lifecycle metrics relied heavily on approximation. SMX introduced a method for plastics, textiles, chemicals, and industrial inputs to retain identity through the entire recycling and repurposing cycle. ESG no longer depended on estimates. It began operating on scientific evidence. Industries that had accepted ambiguity were confronted with a path to measurable truth.

Digital assets completed the realization. Markets built on code still needed a bridge to real-world performance. SMX provided it. The Plastic Cycle Token (PCT) transforms authenticated physical activity into a digital signal that cannot be modeled or fabricated. Digital systems finally gained a source of trust anchored in material identity.

Recognition Came Late, But It Finally Has

These industries did not coordinate their recognition. They arrived at the same conclusion independently. Each sector confronted a weakness it believed was permanent. Each sector discovered that SMX can quietly remove that weakness. Markets reprice when that happens. Yes, partly because of speculation. But more importantly, because the architecture beneath the industry has changed.

This is the part that must be understood. SMX did not pivot. It did not release a new storyline. It did not expand into unrelated categories. It built one engine. That engine, as shown through partnership agreements, is now setting up to power the gold sector's first path to permanent authenticity, the rare earth sector's first path to verified origin, the ESG sector's first path to measurable circularity, and the digital asset sector's first path to authenticated physical input.

Markets set valuations, not companies. And markets revalue technology when they realize it is infrastructure rather than application. That is what happened with processors when the world understood they were the backbone of every modern device. It is what happened with mobile operating systems when people realized apps were simply expressions of a deeper architecture. It is what happened with digital payments when encryption became visible as the true foundation.

The same pattern is repeating. SMX removed structural weaknesses that industries had accepted for decades. Once the engine became visible, markets began correcting their view. That correction is not only predictable. History shows that it's justified.

About SMX

As global businesses face new and complex challenges relating to carbon neutrality and meeting new governmental and regional regulations and standards, SMX is able to offer players along the value chain access to its marking, tracking, measuring and digital platform technology to transition more successfully to a low-carbon economy.

data.title: Mortgage, buy-now, pay-later firms to address US consumer watchdog panel
data.description:
By Douglas Gillison

The U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau will next week convene its expert consumer advisory panel to discuss ending anti-discrimination policies, and a recent proposal to narrow civil-rights-era fair lending regulations, among other recent policy changes, according to an agenda seen by Reuters.

The gathering of its Consumer Advisory Board, which the CFPB is legally required to convene twice a year, follows its proposal at the White House's direction to curtail regulations that seek to prohibit commercial practices that effectively result in discrimination regardless of intent.

The panel comprises outside experts and business people to advise and consult with the agency. The panel will hold those two mandated meetings on Wednesday, the agenda shows.

Current panel members include private-sector and nonprofit advocates for access to housing who have been on the board since before President Donald Trump came to power and overhauled the agency's leadership.

Due to speak at the event are representatives of Chicago mortgage firm Townstone Financial, which settled with the CFPB over allegations of racial discrimination last year, and executives from Credova, a buy-now, pay-later firm specializing in firearms. James Giudice, general counsel at Credova's parent, PublicSquare , a politically connected investment fund, will also speak, according to the agenda.

The Trump administration in August dropped a CFPB investigation into Credova's practices, asserting that it had been politically motivated. A federal court blocked the agency from trying to undo its court-approved settlement with Townstone.

Representatives for the CFPB, Townstone and PublicSquare did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

data.title: US lawmakers press Google, Apple to remove apps tracking immigration agents
data.description:
The House Committee on Homeland Security has asked Google and Apple to detail what steps they are taking to remove mobile applications that allow users to track federal immigration officers.

In letters sent on Friday to Google CEO Sundar Pichai and Apple head Tim Cook, committee leaders singled out ICEBlock, an app previously used to monitor U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents, saying apps hosted on their app stores risk "jeopardizing the safety of DHS personnel." Lawmakers requested a briefing by December 12.

The letters urged Google and Apple to ensure these apps cannot be used to target officers or obstruct lawful immigration enforcement.

The committee noted that while free speech is protected, it does not extend to advocacy that incites imminent lawless action, referencing a landmark Supreme Court ruling.

Google and Apple did not respond to Reuters' requests for comment.

The letters follow concerns that these tools allow users to anonymously report and track the movements of federal agents, including those from ICE and Customs and Border Protection.

In October, Google said that ICEBlock was never available on Google's Play Store and added it had removed similar apps due to policy violations.

Apple also removed ICEBlock and other tracking apps from its App Store at the time.

Attorney General Pam Bondi said the apps "put ICE agents at risk just for doing their jobs," while Apple cited violations of its policies against content that could harm individuals or groups. The removals followed a surge in downloads of ICEBlock, which had more than a million users before being pulled.
